one_line_takeaway: Replacing gradient-ascent LLM unlearning with Newton-style updates whose inverse Hessian is cheaply estimated via block-wise empirical Fisher gives a controllable erasure/utility knob - Fisher Removal for strongest forgetting, Fisher Forgetting for preserved utility across cycles - beating first-order baselines but still trailing full retraining and costly on small datasets.
key_finding: Second-order information yields more robust unlearning than first-order gradient ascent. In the aggregate rankings (Table 7, best=1), Fisher Removal has the strongest efficacy (rank 1.33 on GPT-Neo, 1.58 on OPT), beating both retraining and gradient ascent, but its aggressive updates hurt fidelity (rank ~4.08/3.92). Fisher Forgetting is the mirror image: weaker efficacy (3.71/3.58) but much better, more stable utility (fidelity 2.79/2.63) that survives repeated unlearning cycles. Gradient ascent is efficacy-competitive (2.29/1.88) but highly unstable, with a fidelity-rank standard deviation of 2.69 on GPT-Neo and occasional catastrophic accuracy collapses (e.g. delta-ACC of -21.46 on ARC OPT-2.7B). Finetuning provides essentially no erasure guarantee and sometimes increases exposure. On real-world memorization (Table 8), starting from 16 memorized samples (GPT-Neo/MT) and 22 (OPT/Enron), Fisher Removal cuts these to 4 and 5 respectively - the best among non-retraining methods - while retraining reaches 0 but costs ~10x more runtime (5.69e4 s vs ~1.57e3 s). Separately, the paper shows DP-SGD does not deliver an equally good privacy-utility trade-off across datasets (dominant on Lambada, not robust on PIQA), so it cannot substitute for unlearning.
core_contribution: The paper revisits machine unlearning for LLMs from the perspective of second-order (Hessian) information rather than the first-order/gradient methods that prior work relied on. It proposes two unlearning algorithms derived from the classic Newton update: (1) Fisher Removal, which combines first-order gradient information from the forget set D- with a Hessian-inverse term estimated from the retain set D+, giving a stronger erasure guarantee; and (2) Fisher Forgetting, a variant that perturbs the memory of D- by injecting Gaussian noise scaled by the (fourth-root) inverse Hessian, trading erasure strength for stronger utility preservation across multiple unlearning cycles. The enabling technical move is showing that the otherwise O(d^2) Hessian can be approximated efficiently for LLMs via inverse empirical Fisher, using the Woodbury/Sherman-Morrison recursion plus a diagonal block-wise trick (block width B) that reduces cost from O(d^2) to O(Bd), adapted to a multi-GPU setting to scale to OPT-2.7B. The authors emphasize their methods are data-agnostic and model-agnostic (no data preprocessing, no architecture modification), unlike the cited Eldan et al. (token-replacement) and Chen & Yang EUL (added unlearning layers) approaches. NOTE: Certified Removal (Guo et al.), SISA (Bourtoule et al.), Newton-update KL analysis (Golatkar et al.), and gradient ascent (Jang et al.) are CITED prior work, not this paper's contribution; the paper's own novelty is the empirical-Fisher second-order approximation applied to LLM unlearning and the two resulting algorithms.
threat_model: A service provider has trained an LLM on a user database and must honor stochastic 'right to be forgotten' / copyright-removal requests (GDPR-style) without the prohibitive cost of retraining from scratch. The adversary is assumed to have access to the unlearned model weights theta' and attempts to reconstruct or infer information about the removed samples D- via some readout function f(.); the paper formalizes success as the unlearned model being indistinguishable (KL divergence to zero / bounded mutual information) from a model that never saw D-. Concretely, the practical attack surface is unintended memorization: the paper cites Carlini et al. extracting sensitive strings (addresses, emails, phone numbers, passwords) from GPT-2, and evaluates leakage on a real medical-transcription corpus and the Enron email corpus, measuring how many memorized training samples the model still regenerates from short prefixes after unlearning. Removal efficacy is quantified with an exposure metric (log2|Q| - log2 rank_theta(s)), approximated by sampling from the ECHR corpus.
limitations: Approximating the Hessian remains the dominant cost: second-order methods are O(t*m) where t=|D-| and m is the recursion count, so on small forget/retain sets (when t*m > r) they can be as expensive as or costlier than retraining - runtime is near-retraining on the small ARC (~2k) and Lambada (~5k) datasets and only approaches finetuning cost on large data like MathQA (~30k). Fisher Removal's aggressive parameter updates noticeably degrade utility after a few iterations, and the empirical-Fisher approximation injects extra uncertainty. No method achieves complete erasure of real (non-canary) memorized samples short of full retraining; the authors note strongly duplicated substrings resist unlearning and require pre-training-stage data deduplication (which they classify as preprocessing, not unlearning). Evaluation is capped at 2.7B parameters (no truly frontier-scale models despite claims of scalability), efficacy relies on an approximate sampled exposure metric, and the theoretical KL-to-zero guarantees hold rigorously only for convex models, not the non-convex LLMs actually tested.

datasets_used: Lambada (last-word prediction, 10022 BookCorpus passages; 4869 dev / 5153 test)
Medical Transcription (MT) real-world memorization case study (~5k transcriptions)
Enron Email real-world memorization case study (~500k emails)
ECHR corpus (used to sample the exposure-metric subspace, |S|=1000)
quantitative_results: Overall efficacy rank (Table 7, best=1): Fisher Removal 1.33 (NEO) / 1.58 (OPT); Gradient Ascent 2.29/1.88; Retraining 2.54/2.92; Fisher Forgetting 3.71/3.58; Finetuning 4.75/4.88
Overall fidelity rank: Finetuning 2.00/2.00; Retraining 2.21/2.04; Fisher Forgetting 2.79/2.63; Fisher Removal 4.08/3.92; Gradient Ascent 4.17/4.42 (std 2.69 on NEO, i.e. highly unstable)
Efficiency rank: Gradient Ascent 1 (fastest); Finetuning 2; Fisher Removal / Fisher Forgetting 3; Retraining 5 (slowest)
Memorization on MT (GPT-Neo): No unlearning 16 -> Fisher Removal 4, Fisher Forgetting 8, Gradient Ascent 8, Finetuning 16, Retraining 0
Memorization on Enron (OPT): No unlearning 22 -> Fisher Removal 5, Fisher Forgetting 10, Gradient Ascent 10, Finetuning 22, Retraining 0
Runtime on memorization task: Retraining ~5.69e4 s vs Fisher methods ~1.57e3 s (MT/NEO), i.e. retraining ~10x slower; Gradient Ascent as low as 4-24 s
Example catastrophic gradient-ascent fidelity: delta-ACC -21.46 on ARC OPT-2.7B (128 samples) and -20.12 on ARC OPT-1.3B (256 samples)
Time complexity: Retraining/Finetuning O(r); Gradient Ascent O(t); Fisher Removal/Forgetting O(t*m)
Approximation hyperparameters: block width B=48, recursions m=1024, lambda=1; 16GB GPU fits a 125M (GPT2-small-scale) model
baselines_compared: DP-SGD (compared on privacy-utility trade-off, not as a direct unlearning baseline)
SISA, Eldan et al. Revise-and-Finetune, and Chen & Yang EUL are tabulated (Table 2) but excluded from head-to-head experiments as non-reproducible or LLM-unsuitable
